skill.name: wp-project-triage
skill.description: Inspect WordPress repositories and produce a structured triage report with project type, tooling, tests, versions, and workflow guardrails.
skill.summary: Read-only WordPress repository triage for project type, tooling, tests, and version hints.
skill.author: Automattic

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported network, command execution, credential, and weak crypto patterns, but contextual review found these were false positives. The skill is a read-only WordPress repository detector that uses local filesystem reads and prints a structured report, so the remaining risk is limited to local repository inspection.

low_findings.title: Read-Only Repository File Scanning
low_findings.description: The detector recursively reads repository files and scans wp-config.php for selected WordPress constants. It does not print secrets or send data, but users should expect local repository inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem reads are direct and intentional, and they support the documented triage behavior. Confidence is not higher because repository contents can vary and may include sensitive local files.

low_findings.title: Static Network Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are JSON Schema identifiers, not outbound requests. No fetch, HTTP client, curl, or wget usage was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are schema metadata fields only. There is no code path that uses them for network access.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: Backtick findings are JavaScript template literals used for regular expressions, recommendation strings, and stdout formatting. No child_process import or shell execution was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged code constructs strings and writes JSON to stdout, and the skill instructions only tell the agent to run the detector. I found no shell interpolation or command execution API in the script.

low_findings.title: Static Credential and Crypto Findings Dismissed
low_findings.description: The sensitive and weak crypto detections match WordPress API names, Object.keys calls, path checks, and string includes. No certificate handling, key extraction, hashing, or encryption logic was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged terms are ordinary WordPress and JavaScript identifiers in context. The script does not parse private keys, open certificate files, or call cryptographic APIs.

content.value_statement: WordPress repositories can hide project type, tooling, tests, and version requirements. This skill runs a read-only detector and returns a structured report for safer planning.
actual_capabilities: Detects WordPress project kinds such as plugins, themes, block themes, WP core, and Gutenberg.
Finds local tooling signals from package.json, composer.json, WP-CLI files, and test configuration.
Reports WordPress, Gutenberg, PHP, Node, and dependency version hints when files expose them.
Identifies selected WordPress features such as Interactivity API, Abilities API, InnerBlocks, and wp-env usage.
Suggests likely lint, test, build, and setup commands based on detected project files.
Prints a structured report that can be checked against the included schema.
limitations: It only inspects the local filesystem and does not verify runtime behavior.
It may miss signals outside configured depth and file count limits.
It does not install dependencies or execute project test suites.
It cannot guarantee that suggested commands are safe for every repository.

use_cases.title: Start Work in an Unknown WordPress Repository
use_cases.description: Classify the project and identify the safest development commands before editing files.

use_cases.title: Standardize Triage for Plugin and Theme Reviews
use_cases.description: Collect consistent project metadata before planning fixes, tests, or compatibility checks.

use_cases.title: Prepare Automation Guardrails
use_cases.description: Use detected tooling and tests to choose workflow steps for CI, local checks, or agent instructions.

prompt_templates.title: Run Basic Project Triage
prompt_templates.prompt: Use wp-project-triage on this repository. Summarize the project type, tooling, tests, versions, and recommended next commands.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Safe First Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the WordPress triage detector, then propose a safe edit plan based on the detected project kind and available tests.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Tooling Signals
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the triage report to explain the Node, PHP, WP-CLI, wp-env, and test tooling present in this repository.

prompt_templates.title: Create Agent Workflow Guardrails
prompt_templates.prompt: Run wp-project-triage and convert the results into repository-specific guardrails for build, test, verification, and unsupported actions.

output_examples.input: A repository contains a plugin header, package.json, composer.json, and phpunit.xml.
output_examples.output: The report identifies a WordPress plugin with Node and Composer tooling. It recommends dependency setup and PHPUnit verification.

output_examples.input: A theme repository includes theme.json, templates, patterns, and @wordpress/scripts.
output_examples.output: The report classifies it as a block theme and notes WordPress scripts support for build, lint, and test workflows.

output_examples.input: A repository has wp-content, wp-cli.yml, wp-env configuration, and Playwright dependencies.
output_examples.output: The report treats it as a WordPress site and flags WP-CLI, wp-env, and browser testing signals for workflow planning.

best_practices: Run triage before making repository changes or selecting test commands.
Review the reported commands before executing them in sensitive environments.
Re-run triage after adding build, test, theme, plugin, or WordPress configuration files.
anti_patterns: Do not assume a project type from directory names alone.
Do not execute recommended commands without reviewing local project scripts first.
Do not treat the report as a replacement for manual security review.

faq.question: Does this skill modify repository files?
faq.answer: No. The detector reads local files and prints a report to stdout.

faq.question: Does it send repository data to a remote service?
faq.answer: No evidence of network requests was found in the reviewed skill files.

faq.question: Can it detect both plugins and themes?
faq.answer: Yes. It checks plugin headers, theme headers, block.json, theme.json, and WordPress directory signals.

faq.question: Can it run my tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It reports likely test tooling and suggested commands, but it does not run them.

faq.question: What tools does it need?
faq.answer: It expects a filesystem-based agent with Node. Some follow-up workflows may use WP-CLI.

faq.question: When should I re-run it?
faq.answer: Run it after changes that affect project structure, build tooling, test setup, or WordPress configuration.
